sentences.text: Yes.
I learned-- I had a hard time finding the daughter after the war.
I tried a long, long time, because that was Poland, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Silesia.
It wasn't even East Germany, where you had a little better chance of getting through, you know.
And it was like the-- it was-- in those Cold War days, and subsequently, it was like the other side of the moon.
It was just very hard to find anything.
And friends of mine helped me, Dr.
Kempner, among others.
And then we used Aufbau, the publication that searches for people.
And it was quite a long time before suddenly, a call came to Dr.
Kempner's family that Anna Marie had seen the advertisement.
She had gone.
She had escaped.
She, too, had been in a concentration camp.
And at the end of the war, they got out of this concentration camp.
I think it's one of the classic treks that went across Germany.
Hitler wanted to-- I believe, wanted to get them all into Mauthausen, where there seemed to be enough material to kill what was left of them.
Or they even had plans, I believe, to blow Mauthausen up.
That was in what today is Austria, you know.
And they wanted to get them all concentrated.
The machinery had broken down.
I don't know.
Hitler wanted all the survivors to trek across, and many died on the difficult trek.
But she managed to escape-- I mean, to survive.
I shouldn't say escape.
They got to the West.
They got to the Russians.
They got to the Americans.
And they were free.
And what she went through, I really don't know, but I suppose they took care of the people as well as they could that had survived this terrible march and had been years in the concentration camp.
And to find her, as I say, took many years before I found her.
And then a call came from Chicago-- good old Chicago-- from Annamarie to tell Dr.
Kempner to tell me that-- I was in Germany-- that she had seen the advertisement in Aufbau.
And so I got in touch with her.
And I must say, a part of my-- I don't know whether you can say hard work or stupidity-- I just never kept up with it.
I'm going to, on this trip, make an effort to see if she's still under the same telephone number.
And she had married a man called Pace, P-A-C-E, who also was a survivor.
And I said, what color-- what do you look like?
What color is your hair?
And she said, oh, it's red right now.
And I should go down to the airport in Frankfurt and tell her girlfriend that I knew her or something and so forth and so on.
But somehow, I neglected it.
I just about managed in my bureaucratic life in Germany since-- since the Nuremberg days and through all these years, until I retired.
And then I was busy working for Dr.
Kempner.
I just about managed to write to my mother as long as she lived, you know.
I mean, I didn't get-- I had this big list of things I wanted to do, but priority is priority.
And the army, who was my employer, was a very good taskmaster.
We never ran out of work, you know.
And so I have a bad feeling that I didn't keep it.
I know she was in good circumstances.
She went to her relatives, his brothers and sisters, who settled in Chicago-- or a sister, I guess it was.
I don't know.
But they were very well off.
And they had a furniture family-- I mean, they had succeeded.
They had settled themselves well.
And so she was there and enjoyed her work.
And had no children, but a nice husband.
There you realized what Hitler meant, because the war had started, and you were not so stupid anymore, you know.
And the people on the streets of Amsterdam, and crowding the cafes for their little cup of coffee to talk to each other, were just a bundle of worry.
And we all, with or without much knowledge of the language, we all went down almost every two hours to look at the billboard with all the latest headlines pasted up on it.
You know, England declares war.
Hitler does this, Hitler does that.
He was marching through Poland in those-- I think it was the first six weeks of the war, he swept through Poland and lined up against France.
And everybody was very worried.
You could just feel it.
They all knew that they personally were in danger.
And little Holland knew that it was in danger, you know.
They could see what he was doing to Poland.
And--
Oh, I came back.
As I say, I got here on the last day of October, two-- two months after the war had started.

As we came on a Dutch boat, it was a war boat in the sense that the men-- the painters were up painting the thing neutral, trying to make themselves bomb-proof, that no-- either the British or the Germans would not in any way attack their boat.
And we were very overcrowded.
Everything that could get out of Europe that got on that boat, every other boat.
And we got to New York.
And I think I had 10 pfennigs in my pocket.
I didn't have much money.
Practically nothing left.
My dear family, as the boat delayed, and the ticket delayed, kept sending me a little money.
And I got along all right.
Nothing to do but walk around, look at the bulletin boards, wondered when you, you know, would get the notification from Rotterdam that you could come and get on the boat, you know.
And that's it.
I got into New York.

My father and my-- my brother were there to meet me, and a girlfriend, Rita Barry, who also had been in Germany as one of Ingeborg's classmates and had been with another Jewish family down in Schluctern, not far from Frankfurt.
And I, too, had gone to that family for maybe six weeks at one stage in my stay in the Hamburg language school.
They were an interesting family who fortunately did survive.
And they said, we survived because we have children.
Because they too could have lived independently after their successful soap factory.
He was a big soap manufacturer in Schluchtern.
After they were forced by Hitler to sell it, they, too, could have lived on their income or eked out on their savings.
But we had children, and the children couldn't go to school in Germany.
We had to immigrate.
So they did the-- as good German Jews, they did their very best to do everything to prepare for their going to America.

The children had to keep buying new shoes that were too big for them and to wear them painfully, a day or two at least, so they were scuffed up a little, so the customs wouldn't say, you can't bring those new shoes in without a customs declaration.
So the whole family troop-- they had two dear little children, 8 and 10 years old.
Little girl, 10, and a little boy, 8.
And they had been forced to send their children to Italy to school.
A school, by the way, that Dr.
Kempner was managing after his exile in Italy, in Florence.
But they were all preparation, getting everything they could to think ahead.
What will we need in America, and what can we take along with us?
We couldn't take much money.
Hitler wouldn't let them take much out of Germany.
Not 10 marks, but a certain small amount they could take with them to start out in America.
At that stage, they left Germany in 1938.
And they were preparing every day.
And there, they had a brother.
She was Frau-- Mrs.
Windmiller was a lovely person.
She was one of eight or nine children.
I guess a very orthodox family from the Rhineland.
And she had married this soap manufacturer, Mr.
Wolf.
Fred-- Fritz-- Fred Wolf.
And a well known soap factory.
And her brother-- she was one of these eight or nine children.
She and her brother brought the little ones up.
The mother had died, and the eight or nine all looked to big brother, who was a lawyer.
And the day he got his law credentials going, his little boy was born.
And Hitler said, you can't practice law.
You don't have the right blood type, or something like that, you know.
He was out of the law.

So he went to work at that time for a few years-- this is '38 now, or '33-- he went to work at that time for his brother-in-law in the soap factory-- there's a lot of legal problems there-- and eked out a living there in Schluctern, this little, quiet, relatively reasonable to live there.
And had a little boy, born, as I say, when Hitler came in.
And the little boy was 5-years-old.
And I took the little boy out for walks.
And one day, we went by a big house up on a hill.
And out of this house up on a hill came a group of wild boys, teenagers, 18, 15, I don't know what they were.
Wild and furious, storming down the hill at me and this little five-year-old boy.
And they say, don't touch that kitten!
The little boy had been charmed by a tiny little cat.
A little bit of a kitten.
And had petted the little kitten.
A little five-year-old boy.
And they said, don't touch that kitten.
Don't touch that kitten.
You're Jews, he said.
Don't touch that kitten.
And I thought they were going obliterate us, and I had this little bit of a fellow.
So we hurried our way home, and we didn't do any more walking in that area, you know.
But I never heard such panic and such fury.
As if he had-- I don't know what.
Leprosy or something, and the cat would damage the whole household or something.
And it was nothing at all.
He had just petted the little kitty.
You know, charmed with it.
And I thought, my golly.
I was-- I was-- I was scared to death, because I had also the responsibility of this little kid.
And I understood that they thought we were Jews, and we were doing something wrong.
We were petting the kitty, and that much I grasped.
So we hurried home, you know.
We didn't go walking anymore in that area.
And you will be glad to know the family all managed to get to America in 1938.
And they got along.
I-- I don't suppose they acquired very much money.
They're in South Carolina now.
Some of them, unfortunately, I read about their deaths in an story, you know.
The dear woman.
She studied then, when she got to America, right away.
They all were very eager to study, very intelligent.
She learned to be a practical nurse.
And there she was very good, a lovely personality.
And the children went to school, I guess in New York at first.
And then they must have settled in Charleston, South Carolina.
And the little boy grew up and went to the Navy.
My story?
Well, it was rather meager because there was no job, even now.
So I went to work for the Gallup Poll and things like that to at least get something.
And in different stores, the bookkeeping-- bookkeeping department or something and never did get a teaching job.
But suddenly, Adolf Hitler, who managed our history and my history, too.
Started our war or got us afraid of war.
This was 1940-- I got back in autumn 1939.
And we got into the war in December of '41.
And before that, the government started recruiting us government girls.
And since I couldn't get a teaching job, I learned typing and took a typing test.
And my father felt very bad about it.
He was a very family man.
He likes us all.
I like you all under one roof, you know.
What do you want to see, Germany or Washington?
I like you all under one roof.
But I went away again.
My mother always supported me on these endeavors.
And I went away again and became a government girl in Washington on February 22-- Washington's birthday-- 1942.
And at that time, my brother was scrounging around.
My brother Richard was scrounging around-- five years younger than I was-- was scrounging around to get an education as cheaply as he could.
No money.
Went out to Brigham Young University.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
He went out to Brigham Young University [BACKGROUND NOISE] And was valued.
Was valued by them.
Hmm?
Oh, oh!
Did it fall off?
Oh, dear.
My valuable words, my gosh.
Actually, no, I have a bad habit of talking a long time if I find a listener, right?
At any rate, he was hoping the draft wouldn't catch up with him right away, but fearful.
And it did, very shortly.
Richard Lester.
And he was valued at this Mormon college because he could work on Sunday, and they couldn't, or something like that.
And he could sweep things out, make some money.
Because the Mormons, on a certain day, they can't work.
I don't know.
And he was a Catholic.
He could, I guess, work if he could get any money any time.
And then he even went to Alaska and made a handsome-- what do you call it?
Fishing job.
Enormous pay.
But he said, the cost of living is also enormous.
And then he went to a California plant where they were making war materials.
And of course, he's not a man for machinery or something.
But he said, I watched the man to the right, I watched the man to the left.
And he made these parts, too.
You know?
And he had to do this.
And then went to the army and got into the war and is one of our heroes.
He's one of our heroes.
Because he-- he went through France-- Excuse me.
You keep loosing your mic.
And helped drive-- helped drive Hitler's troops back-- back out of Western Europe.
In the meantime, as I say, I became a government girl.
And we were a mass of people in Washington, all taking Spanish lessons, and all Washington talked Spanish or a little Portuguese.
There were no more diplomats from anywhere.
There were diplomats from South America.
And I was with the Inter-American Affairs Office of Nelson Rockefeller.
And we tried to propagandize better than Hitler did in South America.
And I was in the section that took the Hitler broadcast directed to South America and wrote them up every day in [?
Prezi, ?] in short form.
And then our propagandists would try to see that we did a better job than Hitler was doing trying to convert the many Germans in Latin America, you know.
And a very active German effort in-- war effort in Latin America.
And we were countering that propaganda-wise in that organization.
It was interesting.
It was very interesting work to see what Goebbels had said today and what-- you know.
Well, I did that until-- until I went to OSS, and then I had a stupid fight.
I always tell you these terrible things about myself.
We got a new editor.
Our dear boss left us.
Probably, he went to Spain.
Probably we were trying to influence Spain, too, you know?
And probably because he would either have to go to the army or go to South Amer-- or Spain.
He went away, and we got someone that wasn't so kind to me.
At any rate, it didn't seem that way to me.
And we had an editor that changed Goebbels words, and I didn't like that.
Goebbels had said-- he-- Goebbels quoted the Bible or quoted God.
I don't know.
He said, be either hot or cold.
For if ye are lukewarm, I will spit ye out of my mouth.
Goebbels [INAUDIBLE].
Quoting the Bible, as Goebbels often did, you know.
And this editor changed my lukewarm to tepid, which is a silly word, in my opinion.
If you look at it in the dictionary, you might find it as a synonym.
But I didn't like my lukewarm-- I fought for my lukewarm, and that cost me my job.
I got too 'why should I.' But Goebbels did say, lukewarm.
I've gone all through the Bible, I don't know how many times in my childhood.
And in the English version of the Old Testament, Goebbels-- or not Goebbels.
Goebbels wasn't in the Old Testament, at least.
But he-- they-- they said, I will spit ye out of my mouth if you're not hot or cold, you know.
Be-- be something, you know.
And he changed the lukewarm into tepid.
So I felt pretty bitter about myself that I had, you know, had a bad temper.
Anyhow, and I sat in Jackson Park, and I felt very bad.
I had given-- I needed the job, you know?
But I went to OSS, where they fancied my German.
I had experience.
And I think they might have thought-- oh, I shouldn't say this-- that I might be material that you could plant behind the lines.
I don't know whether I would ever have succeeded, but I could at least help the agency in its office work and so forth.
Anyway.
Very quickly, a columnist for us government people, boys-- government men and women, that we read every day.
You're too young to know him.
Jerry Klutz in the Washington Post.
I see the column is still there, but not Jerry Klutz.
He didn't survive those 50 years.
I hope that he's in good shape now and in a happy, retired period of his life.
At any rate, he said, the government needs, the Pentagon needs right away people who know German to go to Nuremberg.
And so I thought, well, that's all right.
I don't really want to go to Nuremberg.
I've seen enough of Germany.
But it would be nice to have a different job.
So I went down, and I got the job, because they needed me.
I guess they needed some people who knew English, because my German was not that good.
But I took a test.
Very fortunately passed it at the State Department.
And soon, I was on the airplane, going over to Nuremberg with a kind recruiting man.
A very kind recruiting man.
His name was Colonel Leon Dostert.
He was of French origin and was Eisenhower's personal interpreter.
And then he went on a recruiting mission throughout America.
Later, throughout France and England, to recruit personnel for the Nuremberg staff.
And he went all over the place, especially to Milwaukee and places like that, where you find a lot of German, you know?
And I got recruited by Colonel Dostert.
He was so nice and he was a very, very remarkable person.
He developed the thing we're so accustomed to today, but it was brand new at that time, simultaneous translation.
You have the five or six languages sitting behind in a glass cage and each one talking his own language.
And you can-- in the audience, you tune in.
And he was the inventor, the developer of that with IBM.
Criminal Leon Dostert.
He's not alive anymore, but he certainly did a wonderful job for Nuremberg and invented these things.
Well, you see, you belong to a different generation.
My expectations were to have a solid job.
I don't know whether people think about those things anymore, you know.
They say, I want to get into this, or I'd like to get into that.
But we wanted to get where the pay would be regular, you know?
[CHUCKLES]
Well, my mother always backed me very much.
She was, as I say, a frustrated lady that had not enough money and a lot of children and wished so much that she could be employed.
She was not a very strong person.
I don't know how she would have held out.
But she always gave me every encouragement, and support, and pushed me, you know.
In Germany?
Well, from my mother, on the telephone, she said, keep a low profile.
Don't speak up, you know.
She thought the Hitlerites-- the world knew then that Germany was a land of Hitlerites, and she rather feared for us Americans going in there.
So did the army when they went into Germany.
When they went into Nuremberg, we had guards outside our doors every night, burning their fires in the cold winter air, you know.
And we were always under protection and never mingled in any way with the so-called natives, you know.
The indigenous, we called them.
There was a big barrier there because they feared.
They feared.
There were still rumors that Hitler still was alive and that the werewolves were there.
Some sort of magic German people that would some-- take over again sometime, you know.
Oh, a few days after we started.
We were a group of eight or nine people that got onto our airplane with a lot of pre-training; a day or two how to rescue ourselves at sea.
And I was scared to death to fly.
I told Colonel Dostert, and he said, it's nothing.
He said, I've flown that ocean 23 times.
Don't think about it.
It's nothing.
Colonel Dostert, [INAUDIBLE].
But I was scared to death.
But I thought, well, I really should do it.
And I took out $50,000 of life insurance before I got in the airplane.
I thought, well that will do some good, anyway.
And as I came this time, I asked-- I guess it's true.
I don't believe you can take out life insurance anymore when you go take a sea voyage.
They don't want you to bomb the plane, I guess, you know, and get some cash out of it.
But at any rate, we arrived in Paris.
First we stopped in Bermuda.
And everywhere we went, the plane was something wrong with it.
We had to spend 10 hours in bed there, and so forth.
Then we went on to the Azores.
Things were not so up to date as they are today.
We landed in the Azores, and there the soldiers always had terrible stories to tell us.
The last plane-- soldiers.
The last plane-- you know, civilians.
The last plane that came through here, well, it took off all right, but it went right down, you know.
And all these stories, you know, you had no way of knowing is it true or isn't it true.
But the soldiers loved to tell these serious, sad stories to us so-called civilians that were going to sail on somewhere better than they were, you know?
And we arrived then in Paris at Orly Field and went to the Majestic Hotel.
And that was occupied by the American Army.
And all-- we were eight or nine people.
As I say, there was one lieutenant in charge of us, and we had a very peppy middle-aged lady that was older than the rest of us at that time, and she kind of took charge.
She knew intelligent things to do like go to the desk, and find the Army man that will know when the next train to Frankfurt goes, you know, and how we can get on it.
And we got onto a train to Frankfurt that was heading toward Nuremberg, and it took 20 hours.
It stopped more than it started.
It was ice cold.
It wasn't heated.
All along, the buildings were all bombed out, you know.
And everything was in disarray and disorganization.
We got to Frankfurt, and the whole city was bombed out, rubble, and soldiers patrolling before the barbed wire.
And a nice cafeteria where military employees could go in the IG Farbon building, and nobody else could go in there.
And everything very isolated, you know.
Then we went on to Nuremberg in a command car, which is a big thing.
I don't know, 18th, 19th of October.
Ice cold, the wind blew in on us.
We froze, but we made it to Nuremberg.
And then they put us up in the Grand Hotel Nuremberg.
Hitler's hotel in Nuremberg, where he had his big party meetings, you know.
And now it was ours.
And the rest of the city was a mess.
You would see the bathtubs sticking out.
Well, you know, these war pipes.
And the city was just a mass of graveyards of these bombed out Nurembergers, you know.
We'd take Sunday walks.
You wouldn't see a soul.
And I learned later-- I learned a few years back, Dr.
Kempner and myself-- I was taken along as his helper, were the guests of the city of Nuremberg.
And at that time, Nuremberg-- greatly loved Nuremberg Burgermeister told us that 70% of the Nurembergers never returned.
They went out and settled somewhere else, and other people came into Nuremberg, you know, refugees from Poland, Czechoslovakia, all over.
And 70% of the real genuine Nurembergers never came back to their city.
Like everyone else, they had just been dislocated by this earthquake, the war, and just settled down wherever they landed and built themselves a little life wherever they were, you know?
And in Nuremberg, we were sort of kept under guard all the time.
And also-- didn't-- weren't allowed to mingle in any way with the native people, insofar as they were there.
What was your job now in Nuremberg?
Your responsibilities.
I was what they call a research analyst.
I was supposedly knew German and English, a rare thing.
Germans didn't know English, the English didn't know German, you know?
And especially the lawyers were largely handicapped by a lack of-- the American and the British lawyers-- by a lack of knowledge of German.
And the documentation, which was the philosophy, or what do you want to call it?
The style of that trial was documentation.
They've written their own prosecution, they felt, the-- the defendants.
Their documents were behind them, and we found them, great clusters of documents, convicting them, boasting of their wonderful deeds that were the crimes of our trial.
And so these documents had to be poured through.

First found by the Army, which scattered all over Europe and sought them out, brought them to Nuremberg, and they were distributed to-- largely to the so-called research analysts that would then take them to the lawyer concerned if they seemed-- or helped the lawyers concerned if they were puzzled over certain aspects of the documentation, you know.
So that was our work.
You could call it translating and interpreting.
Including the trial interpretation.
But those were the really gifted ones.
It takes a great gift to be a simultaneous interpreter.
A great gift.
We found many very intelligent people learned in both languages.
You put them behind that glass cage, in that glass cage, you can't interpret.
That they think differently, you know.
